coredata.dc:title: Regulation of fiber surface nucleation kinetics via ordered nitrogen-rich carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Guo-Duo
Wang, Zhuo
Zhou, Su-Min
Wang, Tong
Yang, Jin
Sun, Hai-Zhu
coredata.dc:description: Three dimensional (3D) carbon-based current collectors have attracted much attentions as host for Li metal anodes (LMAs) owing to their lightweight, flexible, and wide sources etc. However, the lithiophobic nature of carbon makes it difficult to achieve low nucleation overpotential and dendrite-free Li deposition. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) with nitrogen (N)-rich were introduced to modify carbonized cotton fiber (CCF) coated by reduced graphene oxide (CD@RGCCF). Coating graphene oxide (GO) increases the active site of cotton fiber (CF), which facilitates the growth of CDs on the surface of CF fiber. The introduction of ordered structure CDs drives fast migration of Li+ in the “highway-like” channels and induces the uniform nucleation of Li in the initial stage. As a result, the obtained CD@RGCCF decreases the nucleation overpotential of Li, and achieves dendrite-free Li deposition. the CD@RGCCF|Li half cells exhibit extending cycling life over 5000 h with a voltage hysteresis of 10.5 mV at 2 mA cm−2. As coupled with the LiFePO4 (LFP), the Li@CD@RGCCF||LFP composite anode enables a high reversible specific capacity and improved cycle stability. This work provides a low-cost modification method for carbon substrate to realize dendritic-free LMAs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Lithium nucleation
Dendrite-free
Ion transport
Lithium metal batteries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) The SEM images, (c) elemental distribution mapping of the CD@RGCCF. The CLSM images of the CD@RGCCF in dark field (d1) and compound field (d2). (e, f) The TEM images of CD@RGCCF (the inset is the HRTEM of the CDs on the RGO). (g) The XRD pattern of different fiber materials. (h) C 1 s and (i) N 1 s XPS spectra of the CD@RGCCF.

The Li plating/stripping behaviors on (a, b) CD@RGCCF, (c, d) RGCCF, and (e, f) CCF hosts at 1 mA cm−2. The SEM images of CD@RGCCF (a1-a5), RGCCF (c1-c5), and CCF (e1-e5) after plating 0.5, 1, 3, 9 mAh cm−2, and after plating 9 mAh cm−2 then charging to 0.5 V. Schematic diagram of Li plating/stripping process on the CD@RGCCF (b), RGCCF (d), and CCF (f).

(a) Nyquist impendence plots and (b) LSV of CD@RGCCF|Li, RGCCF|Li, CCF|Li half cells. (c) The CV curves of the CD@RGCCF at different scan rates from 0.1 mV s−1 to 0.5 mV s−1. (d) The b-value of CD@RGCCF obtained by the log(i) vs. log(v) plots at different oxidation and reduction peaks.

(a, b) The CE testing at different current density with a fixed areal capacity of 1 mAh cm− 2 at 2 mA cm−2 and 3 mA cm−2. (c) The nucleation overpotential of four hosts at 2 mA cm−2. (d) The voltage–time profiles of CD@RGCCF, RGCCF, CCF and Cu at 2 mA cm−2 with a cycling capacity of 1mAh cm−2. (e-h) The SEM images of four hosts after running 80 cycles.

(a) The CV curves of the fresh Li||LFP and Li@CD@RGCCF||LFP cells at 0.2 mV s−1. Rate capabilities (b) and cycling performance (c) of full cells at 0.5C. (d) The flexible LMBs coupled with Li@CD@RGCCF and LFP power the LED light at flat and bending conditions and run on time.
The schematic illustration of the nucleation and plating process on CD@RGCCF substrate during Li deposition.

head.ce:title: Regulation of fiber surface nucleation kinetics via ordered nitrogen-rich carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Sustained and Advanced Functional Materials, College of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: NCO School of Army Academy of Armored Forces

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: NCO School of Army Academy of Armored Forces, Changchun 130117, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Three dimensional (3D) carbon-based current collectors have attracted much attentions as host for Li metal anodes (LMAs) owing to their lightweight, flexible, and wide sources etc. However, the lithiophobic nature of carbon makes it difficult to achieve low nucleation overpotential and dendrite-free Li deposition. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) with nitrogen (N)-rich were introduced to modify carbonized cotton fiber (CCF) coated by reduced graphene oxide (CD@RGCCF). Coating graphene oxide (GO) increases the active site of cotton fiber (CF), which facilitates the growth of CDs on the surface of CF fiber. The introduction of ordered structure CDs drives fast migration of Li+ in the “highway-like” channels and induces the uniform nucleation of Li in the initial stage. As a result, the obtained CD@RGCCF decreases the nucleation overpotential of Li, and achieves dendrite-free Li deposition. the CD@RGCCF|Li half cells exhibit extending cycling life over 5000 h with a voltage hysteresis of 10.5 mV at 2 mA cm−2. As coupled with the LiFePO4 (LFP), the Li@CD@RGCCF||LFP composite anode enables a high reversible specific capacity and improved cycle stability. This work provides a low-cost modification method for carbon substrate to realize dendritic-free LMAs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium (Li) metal batteries (LMBs) have attracted enormous attentions as a promising next-generation storage system, owing to the high theoretical specific capacity (3860 mAh g−1), ultra-low electrochemical potential (−3.04 V vs. standard hydrogen electrode) of Li metal anodes (LMAs) [1–4]. However, the high reactivity and host-less Li is prone to side reactions with the electrolyte causing low coulombic efficiency (CE) and growth of dendritic Li during plating/stripping process, which seriously cause poor electrochemical performance and safety hazards [5,6].

In order to solve the above problems, researchers have developed tremendous pathways. Artificial solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) is designed as redistributor for regulating the deposition process of Li [7–9]. Various electrolyte additives or composition modifications are introduced to build more stable Li anode/electrolyte interface layers [10–12]. Lithiophilic three dimensional (3D) current collectors with high surface area are synthesized to be used as the host materials for Li [13–17]. The designs of 3D current collectors not only introduce lithiophilic sites to regulate uniform nucleation and deposition of Li, but also accommodate the dimension variation of LMAs [18–20]. Furthermore, a 3D skeleton can offer high surface area to lower local current density. From the classical Sand’s equation [21], low current densities is favorable to achieve a dendrite-free Li deposition.

Among various 3D framework materials, due to lightweight, flexibility, wide sources and high conductivity [22–24], the carbon-based host materials display a promising application in the LMAs, such as the carbon nanotube [25,26], carbon cloth [6,27–29], carbon fiber/nanofiber [30–32], carbon sponge [33,34]. However, the surface of untreated carbon is almost lithiophobic [9], which is detrimental to the transport of Li+. Thus, optimizing Li+ transport paths on the carbon surface facilitates the construction of stable current collector for LMA. Moreover, the initial nucleation of Li is critical to the Li growth process [35]. Therefore, regulating the initial nucleation process on the surface of carbon is a key step to inhibit the growth of Li dendrites. At present, multiple methods have been developed to modify the adverse Li deposition process on carbon materials. The surface modification of lithiophilic seeds is considered simple and effective method, including organic functional groups [25,26,28], metal compounds [36–39], precious metal [40,41] etc. Nevertheless, involved toxic, high-cost reagents had to be employed. Therefore, the modification of carbon-based 3D current collector using environmentally friendly materials with lithophilic sites is urgently desired.

Carbon dots (CDs) with a size of smaller than 10 nm, such as carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), with nitrogen (N)-rich lithiophilic sites, can be synthesized by controlling the type of precursors. In this work, we developed one all-carbon-based 3D reduced graphene oxide (GO) coating carbonized cotton fiber (CCF) modified with CDs (CD@RGCCF) to as the host for Li. The N-rich CDs endow the CD@RGCCF a high affinity with Li, which regulates the uniform nucleation of Li in the initial plating stage. Therefore, the composite anode without dendritic Li is obtained (Scheme 1 ). Moreover, the CD@RGCCF with ordered structure CDs drives Li+ transport and provides low migration barrier. As a result, Consequently, the CD@RGCCF|Li half cells exhibit extending cycling life which operate over 5000 h with a voltage hysteresis of 10.5 mV under 2 mA cm−2. The full-pouch cell based on the Li@CD@RGCCF anode combined with a LiFePO4 (LFP) cathode exhibits decent applications. The work sheds light on the application of CDs for high-performance LMAs.

The synthesis process is illustrated in Fig. S1 . Due to the extremely hydrophilic nature of the cotton fiber (CF), GO is able to quickly encapsulate on the CF surface during spraying GO aqueous solutions. As prepared CF coated with GO (GCF) shows improved activity[17], which provide active sites for the growth of CDs. The CDs can be anchored in situ to GO, which demonstrated by our previous work. Furthermore, the in-situ introduction of lithiophilic CDs boosts the affinity of CF and gives homogeneous Li nucleation sites. After the hydrothermal and annealing at N2 atmosphere, the black CD@RGCCF was obtained, displaying good flexibility ( Fig. S2 ) and lightness ( Fig. S3 ). According to the equation of theoretical specific capacity (supporting information, experimental section), the prepared CD@RGCCF current collector shows a specific capacity about 2265 mAh g−1.

The interweaved CF has smooth surface with the width of 15 ∼ 20 μm ( Fig. S4
a-c). For the CCF ( Fig. S4
d-f), due to the degradation of organic matter during heating, the width of fiber decreases remarkably and slight folds appear on the fiber. While the surface of RGCCF ( Fig. S4
g-i) displays the folded shape derived from the covering of GO sheets. When CDs were introduced in the GCF via a simple hydrothermal method in situ, the carbon fibers still maintain the integrity and the folded graphene tightly cover on the surface of fibers (Fig. 1
a, b). The C, O, N are uniformly distributed on the fiber (Fig. 1 c), indicating the homogeneous growth of nitrogen-containing CDs. Similarly, to demonstrate the distribution of CDs on the RGCCF, the confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) was tested, the CLSM images of pure CDs show numerous green fluorescence signal in dark field and compound field ( Fig. S5 ). After introducing the CDs, the CLSM images also display clearly green fluorescence signal on whole fibers (Fig. 1 d1, d2), while there isn’t any fluorescence signal detected on the GCF ( Fig. S6 ) and CCF ( Fig. S7 ).

To better explore the location and structure of CDs, the transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) characterizations were performed. The TEM images of CD@RGCCF reveal the CDs successfully grow on the RGO coated on CCF (Fig. 1 e). The HRTEM image determines the lattice spacing of CDs is ≈ 0.31 nm with a diameter about 6 nm (Fig. 1 f and inset), indicating CDs loaded in situ on the fiber surface possess an ordered structure [42,43]. This “highway-like” channels of CDs can provide fast pathways for the migration of Li+. From the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 1 g), before high-temperature treatment, the CF displays three signature peaks at 14.8°, 16.5°, 22.7°, while a broad amorphous peak belonged to low graphitic carbon appeared at 23.0° in the CCF, RGCCF and CD@RGCCF.

In addition, the surface chemical composition of CD@RGCCF was performed by X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). The absorption peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s were detected at 284.80, 398.36, and 532.09 eV, severally ( Fig. S8
a). The C 1 s peak can be deconvoluted four chemical bonds of C, including C-C/C = C (284.7 eV) C-N (285.0 eV), C-O (285.6 eV), C = O (288.0 eV) (Fig. 1 h). The N is in the form of pyridinic-N (398.4 eV), pyrrolic-N (399.6 eV), graphitic-N (401.0 eV) from the high-resolution N 1 s spectra (Fig. 1 i). It is well known that pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N contains extra electrons introducing enhance the lithiophilicity of carbon through adjusting the electro structure. The O1s high-resolution spectrum ( Fig. S8
b) demonstrate abundant O-containing functional groups on the CD@RGCCF. Furthermore, the Raman spectrum ( Fig. S9 ) of three substrates show two sharp peaks at 1350 and 1590 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G band, respectively. D band is associated with defects, while the G band belongs to graphitized structure. From the higher ID/IG ratio of CD@RGCCF, it is noted that the introduction of CDs increases the defects’ structure, which is favorable for Li deposition [12,44]. According to the above result, the flexible, lightweight CD@RGCCF substrates carried plenty of lithiophilic sites and Li+ transport pathways was designed.

To better confirm the role of CDs in the Li nucleating and plating process, ex-situ SEM characterization was performed (Fig. 2 ). At the initial stage, Li is uniformly nucleated on the surface of CD@RGCCF where 0.5 mAh cm-2Li was plated (Fig. 2 a1). As the Li loading increase to 1 mAh cm−2 (Fig. 2 a2), the CDs provide lithiophilic sites to allow Li nanoparticles to be uniformly dispersed around the fiber with a smooth layer. Then the Li was deposited to 3 mAh cm−2 and 9 mAh cm−2 (Fig. 2 a3, a4), tight Li grows surrounding fiber on the CD@RGCCF substrate and fills the spaces between the fibers without any obvious mossy or dendritic Li. From the low magnification SEM and cross side images, the Li uniform distribute on whole CD@RGCCF ( Fig. S10
). In contrast, RGCCF (Fig. 2 c) and CCF (Fig. 2 e) substrate deposited 0.5 mAh cm-2Li exhibit inhomogeneous nucleation due to the weak affinity for Li. The initial Li nucleation process has a significant impact on later Li production. As deposition occurs, it is noted that aggregated Li continues to grow at junctions of interconnected fibers. After plating 9 mAh cm-2Li, mossy or dendritic Li unevenly clustered on the fiber. The monitoring results of deposition behavior demonstrate the homogenous distribution of CDs increase the wettability of Li for carbon substrate, and induce the uniform nucleation and growth of Li during whole plating process.

With the voltage of Li stripping charged to 0.5 V (Fig. 2 a5), a clean fiber surface and empty voids in CD@RGCCF are recovered, indicating the deposited Li is able to strip entirely without any residuals. In stark contrast, irregularly shaped or noodle-like dead Li remains on the surface of RGCCF (Fig. 2 c5), CCF (Fig. 2 e5), these losses of Li result in low CE. Therefore, the plating/stripping processes are summarized graphically in Fig. 2 b, d, f. The schematic diagram reveals that CDs as the lithiophilic sites can regulate the uniform nucleation of Li in the initial stage and induce the Li deposition without dendrite. Moreover, CD@RGCCF has the ability to induce homogeneous plating/stripping of Li, which brings high CE and cyclic stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fast nucleation kinetics of Li plating
ce:section.ce:para:
To elucidate Li+ transport kinetics, the electrochemical impendence spectrum (EIS, Fig. 3
a) reveals smaller charge-transfer resistance for half cells assembling with CD@RGCCF. Moreover, the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV, Fig. 3 b) curves show the CD@RGCCF electrode has a higher current response, demonstrating higher electron exchange rate [35].

In addition, cyclic voltammetry (CV) test was performed at 0.1–1.0 V with different scan rate from 0.1 mV s−1 to 0.5 mV s−1. As shown in the CV curves (Fig. 3 c, Fig. S11 , Fig. S12 ), there is not any reductive peaks and oxidative peaks in the cathodic and anodic process. The rectangle-like CV curves was obtained, indicating that the surface adsorption/desorption of Li ion is the dominant process. According to the CV curves obtained at different scan rate, the charge storage kinetics can be fitted using the following equation:
(1)
i = a
v b

and the equation can be adjusted to
(2)
log
i
= log
a
+ b log
ν

where i and v is the current (mA) and scan rate (mV−1), respectively. Both a and b belong to the parameters. The b is in the range of 0.5 ∼ 1. The b value approaches 0.5, indicating diffusion-controlled process. The b-value approaches 1, indicating pseudocapacitive-controlled process [31,45,46]. Thus, to calculate the b-value, two peaks located at 0.01 V and 0.2 V were chosen in the cathodic and anodic process, respectively. Fig. 3 d exhibit the linear relationships between log(i) and log(ν). The b-value of peak 1 and peak 2 is 0.83 and 0.98, demonstrating that the charge and discharge processes of CD@RGCCF are dominated by capacitive processes with fast kinetics contributes. Conversely, the b-value of RGCCF and CCF is lower than the CD@RGCCF. Especially, the b-values of peak 1, corresponding to Li+ adsorption on the surface fiber during the cathodic scan, demonstrate that prominent pseudocapacitive behavior induced by CDs is favorable for fast charge transfer of Li+.

The CE of Li plating/stripping on the different hosts was tested by assembling coin cells with the Li foil as the counter electrode and Cu, CCF, RGCCF, and CD@RGCCF as the work electrodes. As shown in Fig. 4
a, the CE of CD@RGCCF can stabilize about 97.5 % for 140th cycle at 2 mA cm−2. In comparison, because the weak wettability and lack of nucleation sites of CCF and RGCCF, the CE of CCF appears fading from 78th cycle and drop to 76.4 %. While RGCCF only runs about 96 cycles with a CE about 96.5 % and then decrease to below 80 % in 112th cycle. The Li|Cu half cells fail after 40 cycles with unstable CE. When the current increases to 3 mA cm−2 (Fig. 4 b), the CD@RGCCF retains a higher average CE of 95.8 % for 120 cycles.

The nucleation overpotential (
Î· n
) is the difference between the bottom of voltage dip and the latter plateau voltage in the discharge process [47], the
Î· n
of Li plating on different substrates was calculated. As the voltage discharges below 0 V, the Cu electrode shows a clear voltage dip (∼157.0 mV) at 2 mA cm−2 during initial Li nucleation, and then voltage plateau stays at 92 mV, thus the
Î· n
of Cu is 65.1 mV (Fig. 4 c). Similarly, the
Î· n
of CCF and RGCCF is 18.9 and 15.5 mV, respectively. While almost no sharp tip voltage appears on CD@RGCCF and the
Î· n
is only 2.8 mV, indicating lithiophilic CD@RGCCF with a low Li nucleation barrier.

Galvanostatic cycling tests at different current density were performed to assess the reversibility of four substrates. As shown in the Fig. 4 d, the Cu|Li cell shows a high voltage hysteresis (about 80 mV) in the beginning and then stabilizes to ∼ 30 mV for 500 h. But the cell appears short circuit about 510 h at 2 mA cm−2 for 1mAh cm−2, which is attributed to the formation of Li dendrite leading to cell failure. Similarly, the CCF|Li and RGCCF|Li cells display suddenly increased voltage hysteresis after 365 h and 390 h. In contrast, the CD@RGCCF|Li cell operates over 5000 h with a small voltage fluctuation of 10.5 mV. Furthermore, even at 5.0 mA·cm−2 with a fixed capacity of 2.0 mAh·cm−2 ( Fig. S13 ), the CD@RGCCF|Li symmetric cell exhibits lower voltage hysteresis (58.5 mV) for operating beyond 950 h. According to the above results, the process the decent performance of low voltage hysteresis and long cycle life is attributed to the uniform distribution of lithioliphilic sites and the “highway” ion channel structure of CDs. Compared with the recent works, the CD@RGCCF host shows better cycling performance and lower nucleation overpotential as plated in Table S1 .

The SEM characterization after 80 cycles was performed at 2 mA cm−2 for 1mAh cm−2. The smooth Li appears on the CD@RGCCF surface (Fig. 4 e), implying that CD@RGCCF possesses excellent Li affinity and suppress the growth of dendritic Li. On the contrary, it is clearly observed moss or dendritic Li scatter randomly on RGCCF (Fig. 4 f), CCF (Fig. 4 g) and Cu (Fig. 4 h), which can be ascribed to the lack of lithiophilic sites and side reactions with the electrolyte leaded by poor interphase stability. Thus, the carbon host constructing with CDs for Li is effectively signification for increasing the stability of anode and suppressing the growth of dendritic Li during long-cycling, therefore improving the electrochemical performances of LMAs.

The practical applications of Li@CD@RGCCF (obtained by plating Li on the CD@RGCCF) were further evaluated by constructing full cells with LFP. The similar cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of two full cells demonstrate that they have comparable electrochemical kinetic processes (Fig. 5
a). The EIS spectrum shows Li@CD@RGCCF||LFP full cell has the lower resistance ( Fig. S14 ). The rate performance at 0.2, 0.5, 1, 3, 5C (1C = 170 mA g−1) is shown in Fig. 5 b, the Li@CD@RGCCF||LFP full cell exhibits a decent reversible average discharge specific capacity of 167.35, 152.99, 134.57, 96.21, 63.61 mAh g−1. The discharge specific capacities of Li@CD@RGCCF||LFP cell are higher than the Li||LFP cell at various current density. Moreover, when the rate returns to 1C and 0.2C again, the average discharge specific capacity of Li@CD@RGCCF||LFP full cell still achieves to 132.46 and 167.23 mAh g−1. In addition, Li@CD@RGCCF||LFP displays smaller voltage hysteresis and voltage gaps ( Fig. S15 ). Fig. 5 c compares the cycling performance of full cells at 0.5C. The Li||LFP full cell declines suddenly and fails after 110 cycles, indicating the appearance of short circuit caused by dendritic Li. Meanwhile, the Li@CD@RGCCF||LFP full cell exhibit stable operation of 300 cycles with a capacity retention of 73.7 % (160.5 mAh g−1 in the first cycle and 118.3 mAh g−1 in 300th cycle), and the CE can be maintained at above 99 %. The pouch full cells are able to light up the LEDs in both flat and bent state, and operate steadily for more than three minutes (Fig. 5 d).

In addition, the S cathode was also employed to assemble the Li-S full cell. The fabrication processes of composite S cathode and Li-S full cell are illustrated in the Fig. S16 . The elemental distribution and XRD pattern demonstrate that the S is homogenous loaded on the CD@RGCCF, indicating the ability of holding S for CD@RGCCF ( Fig. S17 ). The obtained Li@CD@RGCCF||S@CD@RGCCF full cell exhibits a stable discharge specific capacity of 938 mAh/g and a CE of 88.96 % after 100 cycles ( Fig. S18 ).

In summary, one flexible and light-weight 3D CD@RGCCF was developed to regulate Li deposition. TEM characterizations demonstrate CDs grow uniformly in situ on RGCCF. The lithiophilic CDs with N-containing functional groups enhance the Li wettability of carbon fiber and effectively reduce nucleation barriers of Li. In addition, highly crystalline CDs accelerate ion transport kinetics on the surface of fiber. Dendrite-free Li deposition is achieved on the CD@RGCCF. Consequently, the CD@RGCCF|Li half cells exhibit extending cycling life which operate over 5000 h with a low voltage hysteresis of 10.5 mV at 2 mA cm−2. This work broadens the application of carbon dots for electrochemical energy storage and enhances lithium metal electrochemical performance.

Guo-Duo Yang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhuo Wang: Validation, Data curation. Su-Min Zhou: Validation, Data curation. Tong Wang: Visualization. Jin Yang: Visualization, Supervision. Hai-Zhu Sun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22035001 and 22275030), Jilin Provincial Education Department (JJKH20231304KJ), Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Plan Project (International Cooperation 20240402073GH), and the Fundamental Research Funds (Science and Technology Achievements Transformation) for the Central Universities of China (CGZH202203).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.152087.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
